results.possibleIdentity: Theodore L. Werntz (often referred to as Ted Werntz) was an engineer and engineering physicist active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He was employed at the Brookhaven National Laboratory (BNL) in Upton, New York, where he worked in the Alternating Gradient Synchrotron (AGS) department as early as 1965. During his time at BNL, he resided in the nearby town of Shirley, New York. Werntz was a prominent figure in the movement for social responsibility in technology, serving as a leading member of the Committee for Social Responsibility in Engineering (CSRE) and contributing to its journal, Spark. He collaborated with sociologist Amitai Etzioni and engineer Stephen H. Unger at the Center for Policy Research in New York City, where he worked on developing sociotechnical systems to facilitate citizen participation in government, specifically proposing concepts for audio-visual conferencing and Congressional Record retrieval. His work bridged the gap between technical engineering and social policy, and he is cited in historical accounts of the period as part of a generation of engineers who sought to redefine the profession's social impact through organizations like CSRE and Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).
